questions.question: In paragraph 8, Teacher Zhang mentions that Ji-li “participates in educational reform”. What inference can be made about his motivation for bringing this up?

responses.content: She is afraid that she will not be educable

questions.question:
Read the following passage from paragraph 10:

“I closed my eyes. I saw Dad standing on a stage, his head bowed, his name written in large black letters, and then crossed out in red ink, on a sign hanging from his neck. I saw myself standing in the middle of the stage, facing thousands of people, condemning Dad for his crimes, raising my fist to lead the chant, ‘Down with Jiang Xi-reng.’ I saw Dad looking at me hopelessly, tears on his face.”

What does this suggest about Ji-li's relationship with her father?

responses.content: I think that he is going to threaten her to go against her father

questions.question: In lines 4 and 5, the speaker says that ”we become strangers in our own home.” Who are the strangers in this home?

responses.content: the parent

questions.question: Re-read lines 6-8. Which of the following inferences is best supported by these lines?

responses.content: That is correct! The speaker is a parent. 🎉
the parent with the teen

You explained that the speaker is a parent. You need to identify who else is included when the speaker says "we become strangers."

Pay attention to lines 1-5. The speaker describes what "they" do (the teenagers), and then says "we become strangers." What does "we" tell you about who the strangers are? Does it mean just the parent, or does it include others? 🤔

activities.text_title: Heroes Every Child Should Know: Perseus

questions.question: What does Perseus's response to the Gorgons in paragraph 49 reveal about his state of mind?

responses.content: The way his actions show how he followed her plan is how fast he took her out and how he did the exact thing she said

questions.question: What does the word “succumb” mean as it is used in paragraph 31?

responses.content: She thinks everybody deserves education
responses.bucket_rationale: The response clearly states that Malala believes everyone deserves education, which matches the text's depiction of her passion and belief in education as a right for all. The answer is concise but directly aligns with the key scene elements from the text.

questions.question: What challenges do the “sisters” that Malala describes face?

responses.content: A. To give in or surrender

questions.question: According to Malala, why couldn't her friend become a doctor?

responses.content: They face a ranged marriages
responses.bucket_rationale: The response 'They face a ranged marriages' identifies 'arranged marriages' (interpreted as 'forced child marriage'), which is a specific challenge mentioned in the text (paragraphs 43-44). This matches the key scene elements and meets the threshold for a correct answer, even though only one challenge is named. The response is text-specific, accurate, and does not contradict the text.

questions.question: Highlight one sentence from the text that best supports the inference that progressive ideas are at the core of many Muslims' faith.

responses.content: Because she was forced  into a arrange marriage
responses.bucket_rationale: The response states that the friend was 'forced into a arrange marriage,' which matches the key element from the text that the friend was forced to marry at age 12. This is a correct identification of the cause that prevented her from becoming a doctor, as described in the text. The response does not contradict the text and meets the threshold for 'correct.'

questions.question: Which statement best describes a central idea of the text?

responses.content: A. Malala has never had the opportunity to take high school courses.

questions.question: Which excerpt from the text most strongly supports the answer to the previous question?

responses.content: A. “But I think the more important question is why shouldn't they, why shouldn't they have this right to go to school.”
